news_summary: Significant progress has been made toward developing highly precise nuclear clocks, marking a major advancement in timekeeping technology.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès significatifs ont été réalisés dans le développement d'horloges nucléaires de haute précision, ce qui marque une avancée majeure dans le domaine des technologies de mesure du temps.
news_summary_es: Se han logrado avances significativos en el desarrollo de relojes nucleares de alta precisión, lo que supone un gran avance en la tecnología de la medición del tiempo.

articles.title: Elusive ‘nuclear clocks’ tick closer to reality — after decades in the making
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 20 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00848-7Super-precise timekeepers based on atomic nuclei could be tested as soon as this year.
articles.content:
Researchers are attempting to build the world’s first nuclear clock. This is a view inside the vacuum chamber that holds crystals doped with the isotope thorium-229, which can be excited by a laser.Credit: Ye Labs/JILA/NIST/University of Colorado

Denver, Colorado

Physicists are getting closer to creating a long-sought ‘nuclear clock’. This device would keep time by measuring energy transitions in the nuclei of atoms and could become the most precise clock on the planet.

Decades ago, scientists predicted that the isotope thorium-229 could be used in such a clock, but they couldn’t pin down its unusual nuclear energy transition. That feat, achieved with a laser in 2024, started the countdown to a nuclear clock.

‘Nuclear clock’ breakthrough paves the way for super-precise timekeeping

Now, such a clock is “way closer than people think”, says Eric Hudson, a physicist at the University of California, Los Angeles, who is working on one. “You’ll see nuclear-clock measurements in 2026, I’m sure.”

Nearly a dozen research teams spread across China, Europe, Japan and the United States are closing in on assembling the components of such a clock, including a source of thorium-229 — which is radioactive — and a powerful continuous-wave ultraviolet laser to excite the energy transition. At the American Physical Society (APS) Global Physics Summit in Denver, Colorado, this week, researchers provided updates on their progress, including details of laser development.

Claire Cramer, the executive director of quantum science at the University of California, Berkeley, who was in attendance, expressed optimism about the potential of solid-state nuclear clocks: “This is a really, really promising technology for commercial applications.”

That’s because nuclear clocks could be resilient to noise and have a compact design for use outside the laboratory. They might also surpass the precision of optical atomic clocks, the field’s current top timekeepers, which lose only one second every 40 billion years.

Laser jockeying

Timekeeping, whether in a pocket watch or a physics laboratory, boils down to counting rapid, regular events — the ‘ticks’ in any clock. In optical atomic clocks, these events are the hopping of electrons in an atom between a ground and excited energy state. A laser with a wavelength in the 350- to 750-nanometre range (the visible, or optical, part of the electromagnetic spectrum) excites this transition, which can ‘tick’ trillions of times per second.

Countdown to a nuclear clock: a three minute guide

By contrast, a nuclear clock would count transitions between nuclear states of thorium-229. These have the same number of protons and neutrons, but different energies depending on how the particles are squeezed together in the nucleus.

For half a century, the precise energy of the thorium-229 transition remained uncertain. Several independent research groups began to close in on an answer a few years ago1. The search culminated in a 2024 experiment2 led by Chuankun Zhang, a physicist now at the California Institute of Technology in Pasadena, and Jun Ye, a physicist at JILA research institute in Boulder, Colorado. Using a frequency comb — a laser with about 30 million frequencies that can hit a crystal simultaneously — Zhang, Ye and their colleagues pinpointed the transition with ultra-high precision. To access it in a functioning nuclear clock, however, scientists now need a powerful and stable continuous-wave laser with an ultraviolet wavelength of around 148 nanometres. And no such laser has been made.

A group based at Tsinghua University in Beijing, China, has taken some of the most promising strides towards constructing one. Last month, the team reported in Nature3 that it had delivered 100 nanowatts of power at 148.4 nanometres. Although researchers have praised the advance, some at the APS meeting expressed hesitation about the laser’s long-term prospects, because it requires heating toxic cadmium vapour to 550 ºC.
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant progress toward building the world’s first nuclear clock, a breakthrough technology that promises unprecedented precision in timekeeping with broad scientific and commercial applications. The story focuses on a major scientific achievement with detailed context about the challenges and advancements, indicating a meaningful technological milestone.
articles.personality_title: Scientists near breakthrough in building the world’s first nuclear clock
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wanted to build a nuclear clock, a new kind of super-precise timekeeper that uses changes inside the nucleus of atoms to measure time. The idea began decades ago but was hard to achieve because the special energy change in thorium-229, the key isotope, was unknown.

**What happened** – In 2024, researchers successfully measured the exact energy change in thorium-229 using a special laser. Now, teams from around the world, including the US, China, and Europe, are working to build a nuclear clock. They are developing powerful ultraviolet lasers and safe ways to handle thorium-229 crystals. At a recent physics meeting in Denver, scientists shared updates on their progress.

**Impact** – Nuclear clocks could be much more accurate than the best atomic clocks we have today, which already lose only one second every 40 billion years. These new clocks might also be smaller and stronger against interference, making them useful outside labs for things like GPS and scientific research. This progress shows a major step toward making these clocks real.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving ultraviolet lasers and building complete nuclear clock systems. They hope to test working nuclear clocks soon, possibly in 2026, and explore how they can be used in everyday technology and science.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After decades of research, scientists are close to creating the world’s first nuclear clock, promising a new level of timekeeping precision and practical uses.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques proches d’une avancée majeure dans la construction de la première horloge nucléaire au monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des décennies, les scientifiques cherchent à construire une horloge nucléaire, un type de chronomètre ultra-précis qui mesure le temps grâce aux changements à l’intérieur du noyau des atomes. L’idée reposait sur un isotope spécial, le thorium-229, dont la transition énergétique exacte était inconnue.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, des chercheurs ont réussi à mesurer précisément cette transition énergétique avec un laser spécial. Aujourd’hui, des équipes du monde entier, notamment aux États-Unis, en Chine et en Europe, travaillent à la construction d’une horloge nucléaire. Elles développent des lasers ultraviolets puissants et cherchent des moyens sûrs de manipuler les cristaux de thorium-229. Lors d’une récente réunion de physique à Denver, les scientifiques ont présenté leurs avancées.

**Impact** – Les horloges nucléaires pourraient être beaucoup plus précises que les meilleures horloges atomiques actuelles, qui ne perdent qu’une seconde tous les 40 milliards d’années. Ces nouvelles horloges pourraient aussi être plus compactes et résistantes aux perturbations, ce qui les rendrait utiles hors des laboratoires, par exemple pour le GPS ou la recherche scientifique. Cette avancée représente un pas important vers la réalisation concrète de ces horloges.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les lasers ultraviolets et à construire des systèmes complets d’horloges nucléaires. Ils espèrent tester des horloges nucléaires fonctionnelles dès 2026 et étudier leurs applications dans la technologie et la science.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Après des décennies de recherche, les scientifiques sont proches de créer la première horloge nucléaire au monde, promettant une précision inédite et de nouvelles applications pratiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos cerca de un avance clave en la construcción del primer reloj nuclear del mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, los científicos han querido construir un reloj nuclear, un tipo de reloj súper preciso que mide el tiempo usando cambios dentro del núcleo de los átomos. La idea se basa en un isótopo especial llamado torio-229, cuya transición energética exacta era desconocida.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, los investigadores lograron medir con precisión esta transición energética usando un láser especial. Ahora, equipos de todo el mundo, incluidos Estados Unidos, China y Europa, trabajan para construir un reloj nuclear. Están desarrollando láseres ultravioleta potentes y formas seguras de manejar cristales de torio-229. En una reciente reunión de física en Denver, los científicos compartieron sus avances.

**Impacto** – Los relojes nucleares podrían ser mucho más precisos que los mejores relojes atómicos actuales, que solo pierden un segundo cada 40 mil millones de años. Estos nuevos relojes también podrían ser más pequeños y resistentes a interferencias, lo que los haría útiles fuera de los laboratorios, por ejemplo para GPS e investigaciones científicas. Este progreso es un paso importante hacia hacer estos relojes una realidad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando los láseres ultravioleta y construyendo sistemas completos de relojes nucleares. Esperan probar relojes nucleares funcionales pronto, posiblemente en 2026, y explorar cómo se pueden usar en tecnología y ciencia.

**Resumen en una frase** – Después de décadas de investigación, los científicos están cerca de crear el primer reloj nuclear del mundo, prometiendo un nuevo nivel de precisión en la medición del tiempo y usos prácticos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing crystalline structure suspended inside a softly illuminated vacuum chamber, with delicate beams of ultraviolet light gently exciting the crystal’s core, symbolizing the precise energy transitions of thorium-229 nuclei, surrounded by subtle abstract waves representing advanced laser frequencies and the quiet hum of cutting-edge physics research.
